faqs.a: Emotional labor is the effort of managing feelings as part of a role. It started in research on jobs that require workers to show certain emotions on demand, and in everyday use it now also covers the often invisible work of keeping relationships and households running smoothly.

faqs.q: Is emotional labor the same as having feelings?
faqs.a: No. Having feelings is just being human. The labor part is the managing, the ongoing work of regulating and performing emotion to meet what a job or relationship seems to ask. That effort is real and tiring even though it doesn't always look like work.

faqs.q: Can emotional labor lead to burnout?
faqs.a: It can, especially when it's constant, one-sided, and unacknowledged. Carrying a lot of emotional labor without rest or recognition can feed resentment and exhaustion. Setting boundaries and naming the work are common first steps people take.
